title: The Best New Bars in Miami
summary: As a gateway between the U.S., the Caribbean, and Latin America, Miami has long been a champion of diverse flavors — rum and agave spirits mingling with notes of citrus and exotic fruits. Recently, vermouth and sherry-based drinks, along with classic Spanish cocktails and…
whatHappening: Miami's bar scene is experiencing a cocktail renaissance, with new establishments showcasing vermouth, sherry, and classic Spanish cocktails alongside the city's traditional rum and agave-based drinks. The trend reflects a broader shift toward sophisticated, spirit-forward cocktails that blend Miami's multicultural influences.
whoInvolved: Eater Miami, the influential food and beverage publication covering the South Florida dining scene, is documenting this evolution. The new bars themselves represent a mix of local operators and regional hospitality groups capitalizing on Miami's reputation as a culinary destination.
whyMatters: The emergence of vermouth and sherry-focused bars signals premiumization in Miami's cocktail culture, potentially attracting a more affluent clientele and raising the overall standard of mixology across the city. This trend also reinforces Miami's positioning as a gateway hub for global flavors, strengthening its appeal as a destination for food and beverage tourism.
moreContext: Miami's geographic position between the U.S., Caribbean, and Latin America has historically made it a melting pot for diverse spirits and flavor profiles, with rum and agave drinks dominating the landscape. The global cocktail industry has recently pivoted toward European aperitifs like vermouth and sherry after years of craft cocktail dominance focused on bourbon and rye. This shift reflects broader consumer interest in lighter, lower-ABV drinks and the growing influence of European bartending traditions on American mixology. Eater's coverage suggests Miami's bar operators are successfully capitalizing on these international trends while maintaining the city's signature tropical and Latin-influenced cocktail identity.

title: Where to Drink the Best Coffee in Miami
summary: Coffee and pastries from Panther Coffee. | Panther Coffee Miami has long been known for excellent Cuban coffee, but the city’s specialty coffee scene took a little longer to find its footing. These days, the Magic City boasts an ever-growing collection of standout coffeehouses…
whatHappening: Miami's coffee scene is experiencing a renaissance, moving beyond traditional Cuban coffee to embrace specialty coffee culture. The city now features a growing collection of standout coffeehouses offering high-quality brews and pastries.
whoInvolved: Panther Coffee is leading Miami's coffee movement, having long established itself as the go-to spot for excellent Cuban coffee. The establishment has become a flagship example of the city's evolving coffee identity.
whyMatters: This shift reflects Miami's maturation as a food and beverage destination, attracting both local enthusiasts and visitors seeking quality coffee experiences. The growth of specialty coffee shops strengthens the city's cultural and economic vibrancy in the hospitality sector.
moreContext: Miami's coffee heritage was traditionally anchored in Cuban coffee culture, a legacy reflecting the city's Cuban immigrant community and their deep coffee traditions. While Cuban coffee remained iconic, the specialty coffee movement—emphasizing single-origin beans, precise brewing techniques, and artisanal roasting—took longer to establish roots in Miami compared to cities like New York and Los Angeles. Panther Coffee's success in bridging both traditions helped catalyze broader acceptance of specialty coffee among Miami residents. The expansion of coffeehouses signals a broader trend of Miami positioning itself as a culinary destination beyond Cuban cuisine.

whoInvolved: Miami Food Pug, a South Florida food news and culture publication, is behind this weekly aggregation effort. The piece pulls together news from various restaurants, food events, and local establishments across the Miami area.
whyMatters: Weekly food roundups help readers stay informed about dining trends, restaurant openings, and culinary events in their region without having to monitor multiple sources. For the South Florida food scene, this aggregated coverage amplifies visibility for smaller stories and local establishments that might otherwise get overlooked.
moreContext: Miami Food Pug has established a Friday tradition of compiling weekly food news that falls outside their main editorial coverage throughout the week. This approach allows the publication to cast a wider net across South Florida's diverse food landscape, from new restaurant launches to pop-up events and industry developments. The roundup format serves as both a content catch-all and a reader convenience feature. Such weekly digests have become a standard practice among food media outlets seeking to provide comprehensive coverage of their local dining scenes.

title: Eating News in Miami, July 2026
summary: A different kind of tea time -- tipsy tea, an unbeatable offer for mango lovers, new high profile restaurant in Coral Gables, ladies night with all the perks, a new waterfront option for cocktails and delicious comfort food from leading chefs, decadent ice cream and churros,…
whatHappening: Miami's food and beverage scene is exploding with new dining experiences this July 2026, from tipsy tea lounges and mango-focused concepts to high-profile restaurant openings and specialty dessert spots. The city is rolling out ladies' nights, waterfront cocktail bars, and comfort food from established chefs, creating a diverse array of options across multiple neighborhoods.
whoInvolved: Miami Curated is reporting on various hospitality entrepreneurs and established chefs launching ventures in Miami's competitive dining market, with particular concentration in upscale areas like Coral Gables and waterfront districts. These include both new concept creators and recognized culinary figures expanding their presence into South Florida.
whyMatters: This surge in dining innovation reflects Miami's strengthening position as a major culinary destination competing with established food capitals. The diversity of concepts—from casual comfort food to upscale cocktail experiences—signals growing consumer spending and investor confidence in the post-pandemic Miami hospitality sector, which has major implications for tourism revenue and local employment.
moreContext: Miami's restaurant scene has undergone significant transformation over the past five years, with major investment flowing into Wynwood, Design District, and Coral Gables as neighborhoods repositioned themselves as culinary hubs. The trend toward experiential dining—where atmosphere and concept matter as much as food quality—reflects broader national preferences among younger diners. Waterfront properties have become increasingly valuable, with developers recognizing that cocktail bars and casual dining with views command premium pricing and foot traffic in Miami's tourist economy.

title: From Casual Lunch to Serious Dinner: Paperfish Sushi Brickell Stepped Up Its Game
summary: Paperfish Sushi Brickell has always been one of my “go to” places to have lunch or happy hour with a friend and they always like it. With all of the sushi restaurants in town you’d ask why Paperfish Sushi? The lovely décor that brightens up your day the moment you walk in,…
whatHappening: Paperfish Sushi Brickell has elevated its offerings beyond casual lunch and happy hour to become a serious dinner destination. The restaurant is expanding its reputation and appeal to a more upscale dining segment.
whoInvolved: Paperfish Sushi Brickell, a sushi restaurant located in Miami's Brickell neighborhood, operates in a highly competitive local sushi market. The establishment has built its brand on creating an inviting atmosphere and consistent quality that keeps customers returning.
whyMatters: The upgrade positions Paperfish to compete with higher-end sushi establishments and capture the lucrative dinner crowd beyond its existing lunch and happy hour clientele. This expansion could drive increased revenue and brand prestige in Miami's competitive dining scene.
moreContext: Paperfish Sushi has long served as a casual dining option for business professionals and friends meeting during lunch breaks and after-work happy hours. The restaurant's key differentiator has been its welcoming décor and positive customer experience rather than competing solely on price. Miami's Brickell neighborhood hosts numerous sushi restaurants, making differentiation critical for sustained growth. The shift to serious dinner service suggests the restaurant is responding to customer demand and market opportunity to extend its business model beyond daytime dining.

title: New Places to Shop, Eat in the Miami Design District
summary: With the crowds having gone from the Dolce & Gabbana show and many from FIFA, now is a good time to make a visit to the Miami Design District, especially since new shops have opened that are brands that are probably new to you as well as restaurants. Plus, you might come across…
whatHappening: New shops and restaurants are opening in Miami's Design District as the area recovers from major events like the Dolce & Gabbana show and FIFA activities. The timing presents an ideal opportunity for visitors to explore fresh retail and dining options in the upscale neighborhood.
whoInvolved: The Miami Design District, a premier luxury shopping and dining destination in Miami, is welcoming new brands and restaurant concepts to its portfolio. The district attracts both local residents and international visitors seeking high-end shopping experiences.
whyMatters: New openings revitalize foot traffic and spending in the Design District during a post-event lull, potentially boosting the local economy. The arrival of unfamiliar brands expands the district's appeal beyond existing luxury retailers, drawing diverse consumer segments.
moreContext: The Miami Design District has historically positioned itself as a premier destination for luxury goods, design, and upscale dining. Major events like fashion shows and international sporting events like FIFA temporarily surge traffic and visibility in the area. The district's strategy of continuously introducing new brands and concepts keeps the destination fresh and competitive with other luxury shopping centers. Post-event periods often present optimal conditions for exploring venues with reduced crowding, allowing for a more leisurely shopping and dining experience.

title: PASTA: The Model Miami Restaurant
summary: Very reasonable prices, excellent food across the board, very good service, low noise decibel so you can easily talk, PASTA is a dream restaurant which should serve as a model to others in Miami. I'll even go so far as to say it has the best pasta in Miami. And on top of all…
whatHappening: PASTA restaurant in Miami is receiving acclaim as a model dining establishment that combines excellent food quality with reasonable pricing and attentive service. The restaurant is being praised for its best-in-class pasta offerings and pleasant dining atmosphere.
whoInvolved: PASTA is a Miami-based restaurant reviewed by Miami Curated, a local food and lifestyle publication. The establishment operates as an exemplary dining venue in Miami's competitive restaurant market.
whyMatters: PASTA's success demonstrates that high-quality dining doesn't require premium pricing, potentially influencing how other Miami restaurants approach their business models. The restaurant's focus on food quality, service, and customer experience without excessive noise or pretension offers a blueprint for sustainable restaurant operations in an increasingly crowded market.
moreContext: Miami's dining scene has traditionally been characterized by high-end establishments with luxury pricing, making PASTA's approach of delivering quality food at accessible prices a notable differentiator. The emphasis on low noise levels and easy conversation suggests a shift away from trendy, loud dining venues toward more comfortable, neighborhood-friendly restaurants. Miami Curated's endorsement as a 'dream restaurant' and 'model' for others indicates the publication views this as a significant example of restaurant excellence. The accolade for having the best pasta in Miami is particularly noteworthy given the city's diverse Italian dining options.

title: A Day in Sunset Harbour Miami
summary: Sunset Harbour Miami is unique. It is one of Miami Beach’s most popular, vibrant, and walkable neighborhoods. Located on the quieter bay side of South Beach, it trades the chaotic tourist energy of Ocean Drive for a chic, wellness-focused, local lifestyle. It packs a big…
whatHappening: Sunset Harbour Miami is emerging as a premier neighborhood destination that balances vibrant social energy with wellness-focused living. The waterfront community on Miami Beach's bay side is attracting locals and visitors seeking an alternative to the tourist-heavy Ocean Drive scene.
whoInvolved: Miami Curated is highlighting Sunset Harbour, a neighborhood community that includes local businesses, restaurants, wellness establishments, and residents who have cultivated this lifestyle destination. The area's stakeholders range from independent shop owners to wellness practitioners shaping the neighborhood's character.
whyMatters: This shift reflects growing demand for authentic, walkable urban neighborhoods that prioritize quality of life over mass tourism. Sunset Harbour's model could influence how Miami Beach markets itself and develops future neighborhoods, potentially attracting a more affluent, health-conscious demographic that generates sustainable long-term economic growth.
moreContext: Sunset Harbour occupies a strategic position on Miami Beach's bay side, geographically separated from the Ocean Drive corridor that has dominated Miami Beach tourism for decades. The neighborhood has strategically positioned itself around wellness, local dining, and pedestrian-friendly street life rather than nightlife-focused tourism. This positioning reflects broader Miami trends toward lifestyle-centered neighborhoods like Wynwood and Design District. The bay-side location offers waterfront amenities and a quieter atmosphere that appeals to both residents seeking permanent community and affluent visitors wanting curated experiences over conventional tourist infrastructure.

title: Taste the Gables is Back with 75 Restaurants
summary: The City of Coral Gables’ popular restaurant month, Taste the Gables, returns this July with its most expansive lineup to date. More than 75 restaurants, ranging from Michelin-recognized destinations to neighborhood favorites and varied cuisines, are participating, showcasing…
whatHappening: Taste the Gables, Coral Gables' premier restaurant month, is returning this July with 75 participating restaurants. The lineup features Michelin-recognized fine dining establishments alongside neighborhood favorites, offering diners a diverse range of cuisines and price points.
whoInvolved: The City of Coral Gables is organizing the event, which brings together the city's restaurant community spanning from upscale destination dining to casual neighborhood eateries.
whyMatters: The expanded event supports local restaurants during the summer season and drives foot traffic to the city's dining district. For diners, it provides an accessible entry point to try high-end establishments and discover new neighborhood gems at promotional pricing.
moreContext: Taste the Gables has established itself as one of Miami's key culinary events, building on prior years' success with increasing restaurant participation and community engagement. The event typically features special prix-fixe menus and exclusive dining experiences that wouldn't otherwise be available. Restaurant months have become a standard promotional strategy across major dining cities, helping establishments boost revenue during slower summer months. This year's 75-restaurant lineup represents the program's most ambitious expansion yet, reflecting confidence in the local dining scene's recovery and growth.
